title: `--dns` option not working for internal networks
body:
### Issue Description

I want to create an internal network and use the `--dns` option to specify the ip address of a container that is connected to the network as DNS server. However this fails.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

First create a test image since we will connect the container to an internal network and can't install anything:
```
FROM debian:stable
RUN apt-get update && apt-get upgrade -y && apt-get install -y dnsmasq dnsutils
```

build the image: `podman build -t dns-test .`

Now create the internal network and use the `--dns` option to specify the ip of the dns server:
```
$ podman network create --internal --subnet 10.89.10.0/24 --dns 10.89.10.20 test_net
test_net
$ podman network inspect test_net
[
     {
          "name": "test_net",
          "id": "5d18abc04cabc13cfaf2c2c77b68435c8bfc0e6917c4eba9f0eaceb661bee591",
          "driver": "bridge",
          "network_interface": "podman3",
          "created": "2025-10-17T16:36:44.611772789+02:00",
          "subnets": [
               {
                    "subnet": "10.89.10.0/24",
                    "gateway": "10.89.10.1"
               }
          ],
          "ipv6_enabled": false,
          "internal": true,
          "dns_enabled": true,
          "network_dns_servers": [
               "10.89.10.20"
          ],
          "ipam_options": {
               "driver": "host-local"
          },
          "containers": {}
     }
]
```

Now start one container that will act as dns server:
```
$ podman run --rm -it --network test_net:ip=10.89.10.20 localhost/dns-test:latest
root@8ceedfbfc802:/# echo "1.2.3.4 test.example.com" >> /etc/hosts
root@8ceedfbfc802:/# dnsmasq
root@8ceedfbfc802:/# dig +short @10.89.10.20 test.example.com
1.2.3.4
```

Now start a second container in another shell and see if dns works as expected:
```
$ podman run --rm -it --network test_net localhost/dns-test:latest
root@27f064de7501:/# dig +short test.example.com
root@27f064de7501:/# dig test.example.com

; <<>> DiG 9.20.11-4-Debian <<>> test.example.com
;; global options: +cmd
;; Got answer:
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NXDOMAIN, id: 52769
;; flags: qr rd ra ad; QUERY: 1, ANSWER: 0, AUTHORITY: 0, ADDITIONAL: 1

;; OPT PSEUDOSECTION:
; EDNS: version: 0, flags:; udp: 1232
; COOKIE: 63ab6d9d76643709 (echoed)
;; QUESTION SECTION:
;test.example.com.		IN	A

;; Query time: 0 msec
;; SERVER: 10.89.10.1#53(10.89.10.1) (UDP)
;; WHEN: Fri Oct 17 14:41:29 UTC 2025
;; MSG SIZE  rcvd: 57

root@27f064de7501:/# dig +short @10.89.10.20 test.example.com
1.2.3.4
root@27f064de7501:/# dig @10.89.10.20 test.example.com

; <<>> DiG 9.20.11-4-Debian <<>> @10.89.10.20 test.example.com
; (1 server found)
;; global options: +cmd
;; Got answer:
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 43001
;; flags: qr aa rd ra; QUERY: 1, ANSWER: 1, AUTHORITY: 0, ADDITIONAL: 1

;; OPT PSEUDOSECTION:
; EDNS: version: 0, flags:; udp: 1232
;; QUESTION SECTION:
;test.example.com.		IN	A

;; ANSWER SECTION:
test.example.com.	0	IN	A	1.2.3.4

;; Query time: 0 msec
;; SERVER: 10.89.10.20#53(10.89.10.20) (UDP)
;; WHEN: Fri Oct 17 14:41:55 UTC 2025
;; MSG SIZE  rcvd: 61
```

As one can see, the dns resolution works only if we query the dns server `10.89.10.20` directly but not by default. If we repeat the above steps but use a "normal" (i.e. not internal) network instead, it works:
```
$ podman network rm test_net
$ podman network create --subnet 10.89.10.0/24 --dns 10.89.10.20 test_net
test_net
$ podman network inspect test_net
[
     {
          "name": "test_net",
          "id": "dacd86f2788741567a009cf421bfd6486c5b567045f8ad2dc9b50af9aad06b83",
          "driver": "bridge",
          "network_interface": "podman3",
          "created": "2025-10-17T16:44:36.712369196+02:00",
          "subnets": [
               {
                    "subnet": "10.89.10.0/24",
                    "gateway": "10.89.10.1"
               }
          ],
          "ipv6_enabled": false,
          "internal": false,
          "dns_enabled": true,
          "network_dns_servers": [
               "10.89.10.20"
          ],
          "ipam_options": {
               "driver": "host-local"
          },
          "containers": {}
     }
]
```

start dns server
```
$ podman run --rm -it --network test_net:ip=10.89.10.20 localhost/dns-test:latest
root@1b8ba248e4ab:/# echo "1.2.3.4 test.example.com" >> /etc/hosts
root@1b8ba248e4ab:/# dnsmasq
```

start dns client (in another shell)
```
$ podman run --rm -it --network test_net localhost/dns-test:latest
root@de62532fe601:/# dig +short test.example.com
1.2.3.4
```


### Describe the results you received

The dns server specified with `--dns` is not queried by default when using an internal network.

### Describe the results you expected

The dns server should be used as it is the case for non-internal networks.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body:
Do you have a default drop rule for port 53? OR have a different dns port configured?
This is most likely a duplicate of https://github.com/containers/netavark/issues/1055 or https://github.com/containers/netavark/issues/1051

> The dns server specified with --dns is not queried by default when using an internal network.

Ah nevermind this is different, what you are doing is not supported because an internal network doesn't allow dns to be forwarded as this is a security problem otherwise. Only the local name resolving is supported and external request get blocked.

I guess one could make the argument that this should be supported when given an explicit --dns argument for the network/container. WDYT @mheon?

> Do you have a default drop rule for port 53? OR have a different dns port configured?

I haven't suspected the firewall yet, because if I specify the server directly using `dig @10.89.10.20 test.example.com` it works. This is why I think it is not related to the firewall. But I can check it tomorrow.

> Only the local name resolving is supported and external request get blocked.

I don't know what you mean with "external requests". In my case I specify a DNS server that is part of the internal network, not an upstream DNS. So there should be no "external" connection outside of the network. However, I think it could be useful if it was possible to specify an external DNS even for an internal network like `podman network create --internal --dns 8.8.8.8 ...` if one wants no outside connection but still be able to resolve names. But this was not what I had in mind initially.

> I don't know what you mean with "external requests".

Well anything that must be forwarded, internal networks means aardvark-dns disables the forwarding of all dns requests, it only answers with names it knows, i.e. only the other container names on the same network.

aardvark-dns doesn't even know if the passed upstream server is part of the internal network subnet so it is simply impossible to make a decision based on that. I think checking on an explicit --dns arg would be the only way this could work.

In general with a setup like that one must be quite careful to not add a dns loop, i.e. the dns server container must not use the aardvark-dns as upstream as it would then loop otherwise.

I'm a bit reluctant here because we originally restricted this as it was a security issue (external DNS servers could be used as a data exfiltration tool). Adding servers explicitly does show user intent, but how do we tell people that this is a bad idea and a security vulnerability?

> I'm a bit reluctant here because we originally restricted this as it was a security issue (external DNS servers could be used as a data exfiltration tool). Adding servers explicitly does show user intent, but how do we tell people that this is a bad idea and a security vulnerability?

We just documented it? We could also add a network option such as `allow_internal_network_dns_leaks=true` to really make this opt in. That may better because one can set the dns servers in containers.conf and then we could treat that as proper opt in and don't have to guess whenever the config file would qualify as opt in for internal networks.
I think a containers.conf option would make me happier, but if other people feel it's unnecessary I won't object to allowing manual DNS configuration for internal networks.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I have a use case where being able to resolve both container names via netavark as well as names defined in a DNS container on the Podman internal network is a necessity. Even if an additional configuration option is needed to opt in, having the capability would be useful.
